lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Now old desire doth in his death-bed lie,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And young affection gapes to be his heir;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: That fair for which love groan’d for and would die,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With tender Juliet match’d, is now not fair.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Now Romeo is beloved and loves again,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Alike betwitched by the charm of looks,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: But to his foe supposed he must complain,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And she steal love’s sweet bait from fearful hooks:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Being held a foe, he may not have access

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: To breathe such vows as lovers use to swear;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And she as much in love, her means much less

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: To meet her new-beloved any where:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: But passion lends them power, time means, to meet,

glosses.phrase: old desire doth in his death-bed lie
glosses.definition: Romeo's old love for Rosaline is dying
young love eagerly waits to inherit — desire for Juliet succeeds desire for Rosaline

glosses.phrase: That fair for which love groan’d

glosses.definition: loves and is loved in return — this distinguishes Juliet from Rosaline
the spell cast by mutual seeing
supposed enemy — Juliet is a Capulet, Romeo's sworn enemy
speak his love-sorrow; plead as a courtly lover

glosses.phrase: steal love’s sweet bait from fearful hooks
glosses.definition: take the sweet lure of love from the dangerous trap it hangs on
she has even fewer opportunities than he does
love gives them strength to risk
balancing extreme hardship with extreme sweetness — the sonnet's closing paradox

annotations.citation_display: Romeo and Juliet Act II Chorus.1–14
annotations.title: A second sonnet — the play's triptych
annotations.body: The Act II chorus is Shakespeare's second sonnet in the play, again spoken by CHORUS and again using the English form. Its function is narrative bridging — Rosaline is eclipsed by Juliet, Romeo's affection is reciprocated, and the obstacles are reframed. But the formal choice is not decorative: between the opening prologue-sonnet and the shared sonnet of 1.5, the Chorus's second intervention completes a triptych of sonnets punctuating the play's first half. After this, the sonnets stop — the poem gives way to the calamity. Some editors (including the 1623 Folio) omit this chorus, suggesting it may have been optional in early performances. The Quarto history makes it an editorial crux; modern editions generally retain it.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)
G. Blakemore Evans, The New Cambridge Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (1984)

trials.prompt: What formal feature does the Act II chorus share with the Prologue?
options: Both are fourteen-line sonnets spoken by CHORUS

trials.prompt: Whose 'old desire' lies 'in his death-bed' according to the Chorus?
What does the chorus establish as distinctive about the new love (with Juliet) vs. the old one (with Rosaline)?
options: The new one is with a wealthier woman
This time Romeo 'loves again' — the love is mutual, not unrequited
It is now sanctioned by both families
It is a platonic rather than romantic attachment
